description:
You are an automation-specialist who has just received two raw log files generated by a workflow-orchestration service.  
Your objective is to parse those logs and create two compact reports (one CSV, one JSON) that another system will ingest.  
Everything must be done from the Linux terminal and should require roughly 5-10 individual shell commands.

Initial environment  
1. Two pre-existing log files are located in  
   /home/user/workflow_logs/  
   • app.log  
   • access.log  
2. You have normal user privileges (no sudo).  
3. Nothing else exists inside /home/user/workflow_logs/ besides the two log files.  

Reporting requirements  
A. CSV report – /home/user/analysis/error_report.csv  
   • Create (or ensure) the parent directory /home/user/analysis/.  
   • The file MUST use UTF-8 encoding, Unix line-endings and **exactly** the following comma-separated structure:  
     header line, then one line per hour that contains at least one “ERROR” entry in app.log.  
       hour,total_errors,first_code,last_code  
     where  
       hour            = two-digit hour (00-23) extracted from the timestamp in app.log  
       total_errors    = count of lines that include the literal string “ERROR” for that hour  
       first_code      = the value of “code=…“ found in the first ERROR line of that hour  
       last_code       = the value of “code=…“ found in the last  ERROR line of that hour  
   • The lines MUST be ordered chronologically by hour.  
   • Example of the *exact* file layout that will be validated (values will of course depend on parsing):  
     hour,total_errors,first_code,last_code  
     09,2,E42,E42  
     10,1,E17,E17  
     11,1,E99,E99  

B. JSON report – /home/user/analysis/ip_report.json  
   • The file must be valid JSON (no trailing commas, no comments).  
   • Content is an array of objects sorted lexicographically by the ip field.  
   • Each object must contain:
       ip              – string, the remote IP from access.log  
       total_requests  – integer, total appearances of that IP  
       server_errors   – integer, requests from that IP whose HTTP status is in the 5xx range  
   • Exact whitespace is NOT tested, but field names, ordering inside objects, and numeric values ARE tested.  
   • Example representation that the automated test will try to parse (spacing optional):  
     [
       {"ip":"10.0.0.5","total_requests":1,"server_errors":1},
       {"ip":"172.16.4.15","total_requests":2,"server_errors":1},
       {"ip":"192.168.0.11","total_requests":3,"server_errors":2}
     ]  

Packaging & final console output  
1. After the two report files exist, create a gzipped tarball named  
   /home/user/analysis/analysis_20230801.tar.gz  
   containing *only* error_report.csv and ip_report.json (no parent directories inside the archive).  
2. In the terminal, print the absolute paths of the three newly-created artefacts—one per line, in this order:  
   /home/user/analysis/error_report.csv  
   /home/user/analysis/ip_report.json  
   /home/user/analysis/analysis_20230801.tar.gz  

Hints (do NOT show commands)  
• Standard CLI tools like grep, awk, cut, sort, uniq, jq, tar, etc., are available.  
• The tasks can be comfortably finished in 5-10 concise commands.  
• Remember to create the /home/user/analysis directory before writing files.  
• Ensure the tarball contains only the two report files and no internal directory structure.
truth:
Pre-existing files before the agent starts:

1. /home/user/workflow_logs/app.log  
Contents (8 lines, with Unix LF endings):  
2023-08-01 09:15:04,123 INFO  Workflow started by user=alice id=WF1001  
2023-08-01 09:16:10,456 ERROR Step 1 failed with code=E42  
2023-08-01 09:16:20,789 INFO  Retrying step 1 attempt=2  
2023-08-01 09:16:25,012 ERROR Step 1 failed with code=E42  
2023-08-01 10:03:55,334 INFO  Workflow resumed id=WF1001  
2023-08-01 10:04:12,667 ERROR Step 2 failed with code=E17  
2023-08-01 11:22:33,890 WARNING Deprecated API used by user=bob  
2023-08-01 11:45:01,101 ERROR Step 3 failed with code=E99  

2. /home/user/workflow_logs/access.log  
Contents (6 lines, Apache combined style):  
192.168.0.11 - - [01/Aug/2023:09:15:04 +0000] "POST /api/workflow HTTP/1.1" 200 523  
192.168.0.11 - - [01/Aug/2023:09:16:10 +0000] "GET /api/workflow/step1 HTTP/1.1" 500 214  
172.16.4.15 - - [01/Aug/2023:09:16:25 +0000] "GET /api/workflow/step1 HTTP/1.1" 500 214  
192.168.0.11 - - [01/Aug/2023:10:04:12 +0000] "GET /api/workflow/step2 HTTP/1.1" 500 214  
10.0.0.5     - - [01/Aug/2023:11:45:01 +0000] "GET /api/workflow/step3 HTTP/1.1" 500 214  
172.16.4.15 - - [01/Aug/2023:11:45:02 +0000] "GET /favicon.ico HTTP/1.1" 404 88  

Expected artefacts after correct completion:

A. /home/user/analysis/error_report.csv (permissions 0644)  
Contents:  
hour,total_errors,first_code,last_code  
09,2,E42,E42  
10,1,E17,E17  
11,1,E99,E99  

B. /home/user/analysis/ip_report.json (permissions 0644)  
Contents (exact, no pretty formatting required, but keys and order fixed):  
[
  {"ip":"10.0.0.5","total_requests":1,"server_errors":1},
  {"ip":"172.16.4.15","total_requests":2,"server_errors":1},
  {"ip":"192.168.0.11","total_requests":3,"server_errors":2}
]  

C. /home/user/analysis/analysis_20230801.tar.gz (permissions 0644)  
  • A single-level tarball that contains exactly two members:  
      error_report.csv  
      ip_report.json  
  • Paths inside the archive must NOT include any directory component.  

During automated testing, the harness will:  
1. Verify that the three files exist at the specified absolute locations.  
2. Check SHA-256 sums of A & B to ensure exact content match.  
3. Extract the tarball and verify it expands to exactly the two report files with byte-identical contents.
